purpose: Determine what work can be sold, delivered, and repeated reliably by exposing delivery reality, scalability limits, execution fragility, and post-sale failure patterns using evidence from live operations and delivered outcomes.

examples: In recent losses, where did buyers lose confidence because delivery capability could not support what was being proposed?
Which opportunities became harder to win because delivery could not credibly support the promise?
Where does delivery capability currently limit what can be sold with confidence?
In won deals, where did delivery inherit commitments that could not be executed as sold?
Which deals exposed a gap between what was promised during the sale and what delivery could actually support?
Where does the transition from sale to delivery break down most often?
Which services perform adequately at small scale but break when volume or complexity increases?
Where does delivery capability fail to scale without degrading performance?
Which types of work cannot currently be expanded without creating instability?
Which work types repeatedly become unstable once delivery begins?
Where do small disruptions create disproportionate delivery problems?
Which parts of the operating model are too fragile to support consistent growth?
Where do current processes fail under the demands of real delivery?
Which workflows cannot support the work being won today?
Where do existing operational processes break when new work is introduced?
Where do handoffs between teams repeatedly introduce delay, confusion, or quality loss?
Which transitions most often cause delivery failure or rework?
Where does ownership become unclear during execution?
Where does delivery performance vary significantly between teams for the same type of work?
Which services produce inconsistent outcomes depending on who executes them?
Where is execution not yet repeatable enough to support scaled growth?
Which opportunities cannot be pursued safely because operational capacity is already too stretched?
Where is growth being limited by resource availability rather than market demand?
Which types of work would overload current delivery if won in higher volume?
Where does delivered quality fall below what was sold or expected?
Which services show inconsistent quality once delivery is underway?
Where is quality too variable to support strong references or repeatable growth?
Where do delivery timelines extend beyond what was committed at sale?
Which operational stages slow down delivery enough to damage outcomes or trust?
Where is speed of execution becoming a limiting factor?
Where does delivery depend on inputs or dependencies that are not fully controlled?
Which outcomes are repeatedly weakened by external or cross-functional dependencies?
Where do dependencies introduce delay, uncertainty, or execution risk?
Which types of work align naturally with the current operating model and therefore deliver cleanly?
Where does the operating model support reliable, repeatable execution?
Which services fit the way the business is structured to deliver today?
Across delivered work, which deals have failed to meet expectations after go-live?
Where do similar delivery failures repeat across different clients or services?
Which post-sale issues recur often enough to reveal a structural pattern?
Which deals become materially more expensive to deliver than expected?
Where does cost-to-serve increase after delivery starts in ways that weaken viability?
Which work types create operational cost pressure that was not visible at sale?
Which opportunities become difficult because service complexity is higher than the operating model can absorb?
Where is work being won that is too complex for consistent delivery?
Which service types look attractive commercially but create operational strain due to complexity?
Where does lack of standardisation make delivery too dependent on individuals or workarounds?
Which services cannot yet be delivered consistently because methods vary too widely?
Where is standardisation missing strongly enough to weaken repeatability?
Which opportunities are being pursued before operations is truly ready to support them?
Where is work moving into delivery ahead of operational readiness?
Which growth ambitions are being constrained because readiness is weaker than assumed?
Which opportunities should be avoided even if winnable because delivery cannot support them reliably?
Which deals create operational risk that outweighs commercial benefit?
Where would winning the work create instability rather than healthy growth?
